title: Mobility
subtitle: Give pieces useful choices
summary: Improve cramped pieces by moving them to squares with more future options.
attribution: Original prose; ideas after Nimzowitsch, My System. No book text reproduced.

steps.title: A piece needs room to work
steps.markdown: [[mobility|Mobility]] is useful freedom. A piece with mobility has meaningful squares and can join plans quickly. White's bishop is shut in by its own d-pawn, but one move can put it on a long diagonal with tempo.

steps.title: Find the freer square
steps.markdown: Move the bishop to an open diagonal where it immediately attacks the rook.

hints.text: The bishop's d2-square is blocked by its own pawn.
Ba3 opens the a3-f8 diagonal and attacks the rook.

feedback.correct: Good. Ba3 transforms the bishop from shut-in to active and attacks the rook at the same time.
feedback.wrong: Look for the bishop move that opens a long diagonal with tempo.

steps.title: What counts as mobility?
steps.markdown: What makes a mobile piece valuable?

options.label: It has useful squares and can change plans
options.explain: Exactly. Mobility is not just the number of legal moves; the moves must matter.

options.label: It never needs support
options.explain: Mobile pieces still need coordination and safe squares.

options.label: It must move every turn
options.explain: A mobile piece is ready to move when needed. It does not have to wander.

steps.title: Improve the cramped piece
steps.markdown: The best mobility moves do more than run away. They give a cramped piece useful work and force the opponent to respond.
